critic_raw:
This multi-task learning design for readmission prediction is carefully constructed but contains several unaddressed structural problems.

First, the multi-task setup uses auxiliary heads predicting 90-day readmission, length of stay, or comorbidity patterns alongside the primary 30-day readmission head. The claim is that auxiliary tasks encourage generalizable representations. However, 90-day readmission and length of stay are strongly correlated with 30-day readmission — they are not independent supervisory signals. Training with correlated auxiliary tasks can cause the shared representation to overfit the shared variance among tasks rather than learning genuinely orthogonal clinical patterns. The design does not ablate the contribution of each auxiliary task, so there is no way to determine whether improvement (if any) comes from multi-task regularization or simply from adding correlated supervision.

Second, the comparison conflates two things simultaneously: multi-task learning and the addition of clinical notes. The baseline is logistic regression on structured EHR data only, while the treatment is a multi-task neural model with both structured and text features. Any performance difference cannot be cleanly attributed to multi-task learning versus the inclusion of clinical notes. The design acknowledges this in Section 5 but does not provide a direct single-task neural model with joint structured+text features as a control. Without this ablation, the hypothesis as stated — that multi-task learning with joint embeddings outperforms structured-only modeling — is not cleanly testable.

Third, 3 years of data (50,000 records total, split 18/6/12 months) yields approximately 30,000 training records. For a multi-task neural model with LSTM/transformer text encoders, 30,000 training examples is modest. Fine-tuning transformer encoders with this sample size risks fitting to idiosyncratic note-writing patterns of specific clinicians in the training period rather than learning transferable clinical signal. The design does not specify whether the text encoder is frozen or fine-tuned, which is a critical architectural decision at this data scale.

Fourth, the stratified temporal split stratifies within each temporal window by readmission status to preserve class distribution. However, stratifying within a temporal window can break temporal structure at the margins: patients discharged near the window boundaries may have their outcomes observed in the adjacent window, which creates a subtle form of temporal leakage if the stratification algorithm forces a fixed number of positive cases from each window.
all_issues_raised: Auxiliary tasks (90-day readmission, length of stay, comorbidity) are correlated with the primary 30-day readmission target, so multi-task learning may amplify shared variance rather than providing independent regularization; without per-task ablations, the source of any improvement is unattributable.

The experiment conflates multi-task learning with note inclusion: the treatment adds both auxiliary tasks and clinical notes over a structured-only baseline, making it impossible to isolate the contribution of multi-task architecture from the contribution of richer text features.

At ~30,000 training records, fine-tuning an LSTM or transformer text encoder risks fitting to clinician-specific documentation patterns rather than generalizable clinical signal; the design does not specify whether the text encoder is frozen or end-to-end fine-tuned, a decision that materially affects overfitting risk.

Stratifying within temporal windows by readmission status to preserve class distribution can introduce subtle temporal leakage at window boundaries, where patients discharged near the cutoff may have their outcomes straddling the window definition.
all_issues_adjudicated: Auxiliary tasks (90-day readmission, length of stay, comorbidity) are correlated with the primary 30-day readmission target, so multi-task learning may amplify shared variance rather than providing independent regularization; without per-task ablations, the source of any improvement is unattributable.

The experiment conflates multi-task learning with note inclusion: the treatment adds both auxiliary tasks and clinical notes over a structured-only baseline, making it impossible to isolate the contribution of multi-task architecture from the contribution of richer text features.

At ~30,000 training records, fine-tuning an LSTM or transformer text encoder risks fitting to clinician-specific documentation patterns rather than generalizable clinical signal; the design does not specify whether the text encoder is frozen or end-to-end fine-tuned, a decision that materially affects overfitting risk.

Stratifying within temporal windows by readmission status to preserve class distribution can introduce subtle temporal leakage at window boundaries, where patients discharged near the cutoff may have their outcomes straddling the window definition.
